title: Coat of arms of Poland

infobox.value: c. 1000 (first version) 29 March 1928 (current design) 22 February 1990 (last modified)
Gules, an eagle argent, armed, crowned and beaked or, langued argent

is the [heraldic symbol](/wiki/Coat_of_arms "Coat of arms") representing Poland. The current version was adopted in 1990. It is a white, crowned [eagle](/wiki/Eagle_(heraldry) "Eagle (heraldry)") with a golden beak and talons, on a red background.

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

| Coat of arms of Poland | |
| --- | --- |
|  | |
| [Armiger](/wiki/Armiger "Armiger") | [Republic of Poland](/wiki/Poland "Poland") |
| Adopted | c. 1000 (first version) 29 March 1928 (current design) 22 February 1990 (last modified) |
| [Shield](/wiki/Shield_(heraldry) "Shield (heraldry)") | Gules, an eagle argent, armed, crowned and beaked or, langued argent |

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/0e/Polish_Eagle_arms.png/250px-Polish_Eagle_arms.png)](/wiki/File:Polish_Eagle_arms.png)

Eagle on the (current) official coat of arms of the republic of Poland

The **coat of arms of Poland[[a]](#cite_note-1)** is the [heraldic symbol](/wiki/Coat_of_arms "Coat of arms") representing Poland. The current version was adopted in 1990. It is a white, crowned [eagle](/wiki/Eagle_(heraldry) "Eagle (heraldry)") with a golden beak and talons, on a red background.

In Poland, the coat of arms as a whole is referred to as *godło* both in official documents and colloquial speech,[[1]](#cite_note-act-2) despite the fact that other coats of arms are usually called a *herb* (e.g. the [Nałęcz *herb*](/wiki/Na%C5%82%C4%99cz_coat_of_arms "Nałęcz coat of arms") or the [coat of arms of Finland](/wiki/Coat_of_arms_of_Finland "Coat of arms of Finland")). This stems from the fact that in [Polish heraldry](/wiki/Polish_heraldry "Polish heraldry"), the word *godło* (plural: *godła*) means only a heraldic charge (in this particular case a white crowned eagle) and not an entire coat of arms, but it is also an archaic word for a national symbol of any sort.[[2]](#cite_note-3) In later legislation only the *herb* retained this designation; it is unknown why.

## Legal basis

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Coat_of_arms_of_Poland&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Legal basis")]

The coat of arms of the Republic of Poland is described in two legal documents: the [Constitution of the Republic of Poland](/wiki/Constitution_of_the_Republic_of_Poland "Constitution of the Republic of Poland") of 1997[[3]](#cite_note-const-4) and the Coat of Arms, Colors and Anthem of the Republic of Poland, and State Seals Act (*Ustawa o godle, barwach i hymnie Rzeczypospolitej Polskiej oraz o pieczęciach państwowych*) of 1980 with subsequent amendments[[1]](#cite_note-act-2) (henceforth referred to as "the Coat of Arms Act").

The Crown was restored on December 31, 1989, by the Act of December 29, 1989 amending the Constitution of the Polish People's Republic (Article 1, point 19).[[4]](#cite_note-5) However, the new emblem design was introduced only on February 22, 1990, by the Act of February 9, 1990 amending the provisions on the coat of arms, colors and anthem of the Republic of Poland.[[5]](#cite_note-6)

Legislation concerning the national symbols is far from perfect. The Coat of Arms Act has been amended several times and refers extensively to executive ordinances, some of which have never been issued. Moreover, the Act contains errors, omissions and inconsistencies which make the law confusing, open to various interpretations and often not followed in practice.[[6]](#cite_note-nik-7)

## Design

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Coat_of_arms_of_Poland&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Design")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/8e/Sobieski_Crown.PNG/250px-Sobieski_Crown.PNG)](/wiki/File:Sobieski_Crown.PNG)

[John III Sobieski](/wiki/John_III_Sobieski "John III Sobieski")'s coat of arms crowning the Royal Chapel in [Gdańsk](/wiki/Gda%C5%84sk "Gdańsk")

According to Chapter I, Article 28, paragraph 1 of the Constitution, the coat of arms of Poland is an image of a crowned white [eagle](/wiki/Eagle_(heraldry) "Eagle (heraldry)") in a red [field](/wiki/Field_(heraldry) "Field (heraldry)").[[3]](#cite_note-const-4) The Coat of Arms Act, Article 4, further specifies that the crown, as well as the eagle's beak and talons, are golden. The eagle's wings are outstretched and its head is turned to its right.[[1]](#cite_note-act-2) In English [heraldic](/wiki/Heraldry "Heraldry") terminology, the arms are [blazoned](/wiki/Blazon "Blazon") as *[Gules](/wiki/Gules "Gules") an [eagle](/wiki/Eagle "Eagle") crowned, beaked and armed [Or](/wiki/Or_(heraldry) "Or (heraldry)")*.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] In contrast to classic heraldry, where the same blazon may be rendered into varying designs, the Coat of Arms Act allows only one official rendering of the national coat of arms. The official design may be found in attachment no. 1 to the Coat of Arms Act.[[1]](#cite_note-act-2)

The nearly circular [charge](/wiki/Charge_(heraldry) "Charge (heraldry)"), *i.e.*, the image of the white eagle, is highly stylized. The heraldic bird is depicted with its wings and legs outstretched, its head turned to the right, in a pose known in heraldry as 'displayed'. The eagle's plumage, as well as its tongue and leg scales are white with gradient shading suggestive of a [bas-relief](/wiki/Bas-relief "Bas-relief"). Each wing is adorned with a curved band extending from the bird's torso to the upper edge of the wing, terminating in a heraldic [cinquefoil](/wiki/Charge_(heraldry)#Plants "Charge (heraldry)") (a stylized five-leafed plant). Three of its leaves are embossed like a [trefoil](/wiki/Trefoil "Trefoil") (note similar trefoils in the medieval designs of the eagle). In heraldic terms, the eagle is "armed", that is to say, its beak and talons are rendered in gold, in contrast to the body. The [crown](/wiki/Crown_(headgear) "Crown (headgear)") on the eagle's head consists of a base and three fleurons extending from it. The base is adorned with three roughly rectangular gemstones. The fleurons – of which the two outer ones are only partly visible – have the shape of a [fleur-de-lis](/wiki/Fleur-de-lis "Fleur-de-lis"). The entire crown, including the gems, as well as spaces between the fleurons, is rendered in gold.

The charge is placed in an [escutcheon](/wiki/Escutcheon_(heraldry) "Escutcheon (heraldry)") (shield) of the Modern French type. It is a nearly rectangular upright [isosceles trapezoid](/wiki/Isosceles_trapezoid "Isosceles trapezoid"), rounded at the bottom, whose upper base is slightly longer than the lower one, from the middle of which extends downwards a pointed tip. Although the shield is an integral part of the coat of arms, Polish law stipulates, in certain cases, to only use the charge without the escutcheon. The shades of the principal tinctures, white ([Argent](/wiki/Argent "Argent")) and red ([Gules](/wiki/Gules "Gules")), which are the national colors of Poland, are specified as coordinates in the [CIE 1976](/wiki/Lab_color_space "Lab color space") color space (see [Flag of Poland – National colors](/wiki/Flag_of_Poland#National_colors "Flag of Poland") for details).

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Coat_of_arms_of_Poland&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: History")]

According to legend, the White Eagle emblem originated when Poland's legendary founder [Lech](/wiki/Lech,_Czech_and_Rus "Lech, Czech and Rus") saw a white eagle's nest.[[7]](#cite_note-8) When he looked at the bird, a ray of sunshine from the red setting sun fell on its wings, so they appeared tipped with gold, the rest of the eagle was pure white. He was delighted and decided to settle there and placed the eagle on his emblem. He also named the place Gniezdno (currently [Gniezno](/wiki/Gniezno "Gniezno")) from the Polish word *gniazdo* ("nest").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/60/Denar_rys_chrobry1.png/250px-Denar_rys_chrobry1.png)](/wiki/File:Denar_rys_chrobry1.png)

[Chrobry](/wiki/Boles%C5%82aw_I_the_Brave "Bolesław I the Brave") [denarius](/wiki/Denarius "Denarius") with a heraldic bird, about 1000 AD

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/6b/Tapestry_with_the_coats_of_arms_of_Poland_%28White_Eagle%29_and_Lithuania_%28Vytis%2C_Pahonia%29_and_a_figure_of_Victory.jpg/250px-Tapestry_with_the_coats_of_arms_of_Poland_%28White_Eagle%29_and_Lithuania_%28Vytis%2C_Pahonia%29_and_a_figure_of_Victory.jpg)](/wiki/File:Tapestry_with_the_coats_of_arms_of_Poland_(White_Eagle)_and_Lithuania_(Vytis,_Pahonia)_and_a_figure_of_Victory.jpg)

Tapestry with the coats of arms of the [Crown of the Kingdom of Poland](/wiki/Crown_of_the_Kingdom_of_Poland "Crown of the Kingdom of Poland") and Lithuania, c. 1555

The symbol of an eagle appeared for the first time on the coins made during the reign of [Bolesław I](/wiki/Boles%C5%82aw_I_the_Brave "Bolesław I the Brave") (992–1025), initially as the coat of arms of the [Piast dynasty](/wiki/Piast_dynasty "Piast dynasty"). Beginning in the 12th century, the eagle has appeared on the shields, ensigns, coins, and seals of the Piast dukes. It appeared on the Polish coat of arms during [Przemysł II](/wiki/Przemys%C5%82_II "Przemysł II") reign as a reminder of the Piast tradition before the fragmentation of Poland.

The eagle's graphic form has changed throughout centuries. Its recent shape, accepted in 1927, was designed by professor [Zygmunt Kamiński](/wiki/Zygmunt_Kami%C5%84ski_(painter) "Zygmunt Kamiński (painter)")[[8]](#cite_note-9) and was based on the eagle's form from the times of [Stefan Batory](/wiki/Stefan_Batory "Stefan Batory")'s reign. It was adapted to stamps or round shields rather than to a rectangular shape.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f6/Kremlin_Armoury_2.jpg/250px-Kremlin_Armoury_2.jpg)](/wiki/File:Kremlin_Armoury_2.jpg)

A silver heraldic base for King [John Casimir](/wiki/John_II_Casimir_of_Poland "John II Casimir of Poland")'s [crown](/wiki/Polish_Crown_Jewels "Polish Crown Jewels"), c. 1666

The arms of the [Polish–Lithuanian Commonwealth](/wiki/Polish%E2%80%93Lithuanian_Commonwealth "Polish–Lithuanian Commonwealth") was quartered, with Polish eagle and [Lithuanian](/wiki/Lithuania "Lithuania") [Pogonia](/wiki/Coat_of_arms_of_Lithuania "Coat of arms of Lithuania") on opposite sides. Kings used to place their own emblems in escutcheon point (i.e., House Vasa).

Despite the fact that new emblems were given to provinces established by the invaders after the [partitions of Poland](/wiki/Partitions_of_Poland "Partitions of Poland"), the White Eagle remained there with or without crown and occasionally with face turned towards left and in some exceptions with Pogonia. But in most cases they were combined with the invader's emblem.

The Poles conscientiously collected coins from the pre-partitions period with the eagle on their obverse and reverse. The symbol of the eagle, often with [Pogonia](/wiki/Coat_of_arms_of_Lithuania "Coat of arms of Lithuania"), appeared on numerous flags and emblems of the [November Uprising](/wiki/November_Uprising "November Uprising").

The resurrection of the [Polish Kingdom](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Poland_(1916%E2%80%931918) "Kingdom of Poland (1916–1918)") (Polish Regency) in the territories of the former [Congress Poland](/wiki/Congress_Poland "Congress Poland") (which had been partitioned and annexed by the [Russian Empire](/wiki/Russian_Empire "Russian Empire") as the [Vistula Land](/wiki/Vistula_Land "Vistula Land") in 1867) was approved by [Austria-Hungary](/wiki/Austria-Hungary "Austria-Hungary") and [Wilhelm II](/wiki/Wilhelm_II,_German_Emperor "Wilhelm II, German Emperor")'s Germany in 1916. A year later, the first Polish banknotes (Polish Marka) with Crowned Eagle on an indivisible shield were introduced. After regaining total independence and the creation of the [Second Polish Republic](/wiki/Second_Polish_Republic "Second Polish Republic") (1918–1939) the White Eagle was implemented by the act of 1919. The official image of the coat of arms (which resembled the emblem of [Stanislaus Augustus](/wiki/Stanislaus_Augustus "Stanislaus Augustus")) was redesigned in 1927 by Zygmunt Kamiński. This design was introduced by law on 13 December of that year before coming into effect on 29 March 1928.[[9]](#cite_note-10) According to the research of [Polish](/wiki/Poland "Poland") [heraldist](/wiki/Heraldist "Heraldist") Jerzy Michta published in 2017, the version designed by Kamiński was actually plagiarized from a 1924 medal by [Elisa Beetz-Charpentier](/wiki/Elisa_Beetz-Charpentier "Elisa Beetz-Charpentier") made in honor of [Ignacy Paderewski](/wiki/Ignacy_Paderewski "Ignacy Paderewski").[[10]](#cite_note-RP-11)

After [World War II](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II"), the communist authorities of the [Polish People's Republic](/wiki/Polish_People%27s_Republic "Polish People's Republic") removed the crown from the eagle's head. Still, Poland was one of the few countries in the Eastern Bloc with no communist symbols (red stars, ears of wheat, hammers, etc.) on either its flag or its coat of arms. The crownless design was approved by resolution in 1955. To counter that, the Polish government in exile introduced a new emblem with a cross added atop the crown. After the fall of communism in 1989, the coat of arms was swiftly redesigned by [Andrzej Heidrich](/wiki/Andrzej_Heidrich "Andrzej Heidrich") using the Kamiński's design as a basis. The modifications include the removal of the yellow border around the shield and changing the cinquefoils that adorned the upper edges of the eagle's wings from resembling stars to be in the shape of a trefoil.[[11]](#cite_note-12) The crown was also returned to the eagle's head. The redesigned coat of arms was adopted by law on 22 February 1990.[[12]](#cite_note-13)

The eagle appears on many public administration buildings, it is present in schools and courts. Furthermore, it is placed on the obverse of [Polish coins](/wiki/Coins_of_Poland "Coins of Poland"). However the issue on which conditions it should be exposed and how it should be interpreted is the topic of numerous debates in Poland. The eagle was formerly on the [Poland national football team](/wiki/Poland_national_football_team "Poland national football team")'s shirts; a new shirt without the eagle was introduced in November 2011, prompting complaints from fans and president [Bronisław Komorowski](/wiki/Bronis%C5%82aw_Komorowski "Bronisław Komorowski"). Due to this overwhelming public pressure, the football shirts were redesigned with the eagle reinstated in the centre of the shirt in December 2011.[[13]](#cite_note-14)

#### Communist Poland

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Coat_of_arms_of_Poland&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Communist Poland")]

Used since 1944 and legalized in 1952, the emblem of the Polish People's Republic was valid until February 21, 1990. In addition to the lack of a crown and other rosettes, there is a significant deformation of the eagle's head and eye compared to the pre-war eagle.[[15]](#cite_note-16)

* [![The coat of arms of the Polish People's Republic (1944–1990) according to the law.](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/af/Herb_prl_1980.png/120px-Herb_prl_1980.png)](/wiki/File:Herb_prl_1980.png "The coat of arms of the Polish People's Republic (1944–1990) according to the law.")

  The coat of arms of the Polish People's Republic (1944–1990) according to the law.
* [![The coat of arms of the Polish People's Republic against the Palace of Culture and Science.](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/0e/PL_Wwa%2C_PKiN%2C_z_orlem_trybuny%2C_2010.06.13_%282%29.jpg/120px-PL_Wwa%2C_PKiN%2C_z_orlem_trybuny%2C_2010.06.13_%282%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:PL_Wwa,_PKiN,_z_orlem_trybuny,_2010.06.13_(2).jpg "The coat of arms of the Polish People's Republic against the Palace of Culture and Science.")

  The coat of arms of the Polish People's Republic against the [Palace of Culture and Science](/wiki/Palace_of_Culture_and_Science "Palace of Culture and Science").

#### Third Polish Republic

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Coat_of_arms_of_Poland&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Third Polish Republic")]

The current version of the Emblem is consistent with the Act of February 22, 1990. Significant modifications by Andrzej Heidrich are visible compared to the pre-war eagle created by Zygmunt Kamiński.[[16]](#cite_note-17)
